using a Shimadzu AG-Xplus1 00kN tensile testing machine with a rate of 0.5 mm/min. At least three specimens were tested for each condition to ensure validity .
The specimens for optical observation (Zeiss AXIO) were further etched with Kroll’s solution of 4% hydrofluoric acid, 10% nitric acid, and 86% deionized water.
The in-situ microstructural evolution and ex-situ microstructure details were analyzed by scanning electron microscope (Zeiss Gemini 300) with electron backscatter diffraction (EBSD, Symmetry).
EBSD analysis was carried out at an accelerating voltage of 20 kV and an acquisition rate of 383.64 Hz.
The element distribution was analyzed by a transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEM-2100 F) equipped with an energy dispersive spectrometer (EDS), and the voltage was 200 kV.
The XRD (Smart Lab) tests were carried out using Cu-Kα radiation at an accelerating voltage of 40 kV, current of 30 mA, and scan range between 30° and 90° with a scan rate of 6°/min.

The micromechanical properties of different phases were examined using a nanoindentation facility (Bruker Hysitron TI980) equipped with a scanning probe microscope (SPM), and more than 5 indentations were conducted at each phase to achieve statistically valid data. All measurements were conducted with a fixed depth of 50–80 nm.

evidence: Ti–3Al–6Fe–6V–2Zr, wt%
This alloy contained 3 % aluminum (Al), 6 % vanadium (V), 6 % iron (Fe), and 2 % zirconium (Zr) and was designated as Ti–3Al–6V–6Fe–2Zr (Ti3662).
We found that 3 wt% Al was sufficient to achieve a microstructure that balances high strength and decent ductility after aging treatment.
Vanadium (V), a typical isomorphous β-stabilizer, was included at 6 wt% to maintain β-phase stability and prevent stress-induced phase transformation during loading.
zirconium (Zr), which strengthens the α-phase and aids β-phase stabilization by suppressing ω-phase formation, was introduced at 2 wt%

A Ti-55511 alloy forged bar was used as the raw material in this study
Three cuboid samples (150 mm × 45 mm × 15 mm) were cut from the forged-bar by wire-cut discharge machining
cross-rolled (as reported previously by us [17]) in the β field by a series of passes that each reducing the total thickness of the sample by 5%
After each pass, the samples were placed in the furnace again for a dwell time of 3 min to maintain the rolling temperature
Once the samples were reduced to 20% of their original thickness, they were quenched in ice water
the special dislocation array produced by cross-rolling
hot-rolled near-β Ti alloys
the single β phase was previously cross-rolled
α-Twinning occurred during heat treatment and continuously evolved with the ongoing heat treatment time
These samples were first annealed at 920°C for 30 min to acquire the full β microstructure
The three cross-rolled samples were finally heat treated at 600°C for 15 min, 30 min, and 60 min to evaluate the effects of heat treatment time on microstructure

The mechanical properties of TVMF alloy rolled at different temperatures were assessed through uniaxial tensile tests at room temperature.
deformed by hot rolling
hot forging
hot rolling processes
Flat dog-bone-shaped specimens with a standard pitch section of 13 mm ×4 mm ×2 mm were utilized for the tests.
Tensile testing was performed using an Instron 5982 machine at a strain rate of 5 ×10⁻⁴ m/s, with strain monitored throughout the process via a contact extensometer.
The direction of tensile testing was parallel to the rolling direction.
Each group was tested three times to avoid error in results.
XRD analyses were conducted within a range of 20°–80° with a step size of 0.02°/step/s.

parameters.sample_preparation: mechanical polishing followed by electrolytic polishing

evidence: Microstructural evolution was analyzed using electron backscatter diffraction (EBSD, Oxford, RILI su 5000), operating at a voltage of 30 kV with an EBSD test step size of 0.4 μm.
Samples preparation for EBSD involved mechanical polishing followed by electrolytic polishing.
Electrolytic polishing was carried out using a mixture of 1% perchloric acid and 9% methanol at a voltage of 20 V and a current ranging from 0.3 to 0.5 A.
Additionally, the microstructure of the alloy was examined via transmission electron microscopy (TEM) using an FEI Talos F200X instrument.
The TEM samples with a thickness of approximately 30 μm and a diameter of 3 mm, were prepared through mechanical polishing and twin-jet electropolishing.
The twin-jet polishing was performed at -15 °C using a 1% perchloric acid and 9% methanol etching solution.
The results of EBSD show that the direction of activated twin variants in G1 and G2 grains was consistent with the trace lines direction of the larger value of SF.

causal_relations_full.effect: precipitation of fine lamellar αs grains

causal_relations_full.effect: reduces the driving force for the precipitation of lamellar αs grains and provides sufficient time for coarsening

causal_relations_full.cause: precipitation of lamellar αs grains becomes increasingly difficult

causal_relations_full.effect: sufficiently high volume fraction of high-temperature β-phase
evidence: an increase in annealing temperature ensures a sufficiently high volume fraction of high-temperature β-phase

causal_relations_full.cause: abundant high-temperature β-phase during rapid cooling

causal_relations_full.cause: sufficient insulation time during continuous cooling

causal_relations_full.effect: growth of equiaxed α-phase and variations in solute element concentrations
evidence: As the equiaxed α-phase continuously grows during cooling, elemental diffusion leads to variations in solute element concentrations within different regions of the equiaxed α-phase.

causal_relations_full.cause: diffusion model does not consider synergistic interactions among various elements

evidence: This discrepancy arises because the diffusion model does not consider the synergistic interactions among various elements.

causal_relations_full.cause: diffusion coefficients of the four controlling elements differ

causal_relations_full.effect: low volume fraction of high-temperature β-phase and high concentration of β-stabilizing elements within β-phase

causal_relations_full.effect: high volume fraction of high-temperature β-phase and low concentration of β-stabilizing elements within β-phase
evidence: when the annealing temperature is high, the volume fraction of the high-temperature β-phase is high, and the concentration of β-stabilizing elements within the β-phase is relatively low.

causal_relations_full.cause: low annealing temperature and sufficient element diffusion needed
causal_relations_full.effect: growth of equiaxed α-phase determined by controlling elements with low diffusion coefficients

causal_relations_full.cause: high annealing temperature and only small amount of element diffusion required
causal_relations_full.effect: growth of equiaxed α-phase determined by controlling elements with high diffusion coefficients
evidence: The growth of the equiaxed α-phase is determined by the controlling elements with high diffusion coefficients.

HfNbTaTiZr is a typical bcc HEA
Phase constituent of HfNbTaTiZr alloys was examined by an X-ray diffractometry (XRD) with CuKα radiation.
Microstructure of the polycrystals before and after the homogenization is shown in Fig. 1.
The precipitation of the ω phase is observed for the first time in HfNbTaTiZr alloys.
Thus, the microstructure of HfNbTaTiZr alloys depends strongly on annealing temperature.
This suggests that 1/2D111Fscrew dislocations are responsible for the deformation behavior of HfNbTaTiZr alloys, similar to conventional bcc metals.

The single crystalline specimens could be obtained from the coarse-grained polycrystals annealed just below the melting point.

parameters.temperature_range: up to 673 K, at 873 K, further increase

evidence: The yield stress decreased rapidly with increasing temperature up to 673 K, while the stress increased at 873 K. Further increase in temperature resulted in a decrease in yield stress.
The strain-rate sensitivity of the deformation behavior
micro-Vickers hardness after the heat treatment

Compression tests for the ST single crystals and polycrystals were performed in the temperature range from 93 K to 1273 K at a cross-head speed of 0.05 mm/min corresponding to an initial strain rate (ε≡) of 1.7 × 10⁻⁴/s.
In the tests, ε≡was abruptly changed at an interval of a plastic strain (ε) of 1% in the following manner: 1.7 ×10⁻⁴/s → 1.7 ×10⁻⁵/s → 1.7 ×10⁻⁴/s → 1.7 ×10⁻³/s → 1.7 ×10⁻⁴/s.
Tensile tests were also performed with ε≡1.7 ×10⁻⁴/s at room temperature.
Micro-Vickers hardness of the specimens was measured at 9.807 N for 15 s to examine the effect of the phase transformation on the mechanical properties.
Phase constituent of HfNbTaTiZr alloys was examined by an X-ray diffractometry (XRD) with CuKα radiation.

Microstructure of the specimens were observed using a field-emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM) equipped with back-scattered electron (BSE) and electron backscattered diffraction (EBSD) detectors.
The chemical composition of the constituent phases in the alloys was measured by an energy-dispersive X-ray spectrometry (EDS).
The slip planes after compression were determined by a two-surface trace analysis using an optical microscope.
The microstructure and dislocation structure were observed using a transmission electron microscope (TEM) operated at 300 kV.
XRD patterns shown in Fig. 2 also suggest that the hcp phase is precipitated at 773–973 K

at 673 K, there are extra spots in the TEM diffraction pattern with a beam direction (B) of [101] of the bcc phase and the fine precipitates can be seen in the dark field image of the extra spot (Fig. 6)
In the polycrystals, the dislocations are visible with a reflection vector (g) of 101 (Fig. 9 (a)) while are out of contrast with 121 (Fig. 9 (b)) and 011 (Fig. 9 (c)).
the critical resolved shear stress for observed D111Fslip in the single crystals compressed at room temperature is calculated to be 449 MPa
the strain-rate change tests were performed for the ST polycrystals (Fig. 14) and single crystals (Fig. 15)

causal_relations_full.cause: dislocations spontaneously rearranging at elevated temperatures
causal_relations_full.effect: formation of dislocation walls and sub-grain boundaries (polygonization)
evidence: This is because dislocations can spontaneously rearrange themselves at elevated temperatures to decrease the total energy by forming dislocation walls and sub-grain boundaries, a so-called polygonization structure

causal_relations_full.cause: sub-grain boundaries splitting primary α laths after intersecting with α/β interfaces

causal_relations_full.effect: α nuclei are encompassed by β phase
evidence: Upon long-term annealing at high temperatures, the separated and undissolved α nuclei are encompassed by β phase

causal_relations_full.cause: epitaxial growth of α nuclei in β matrix during continuous cooling
causal_relations_full.effect: formation of globular α grains
evidence: these α nuclei grow in the β matrix through epitaxial growth in the following continuous cooling process, resulting in globular α grains

causal_relations_full.cause: heating and cooling cycle inducing oscillation of α and β volume fractions
causal_relations_full.effect: near complete globularization upon slow cooling
evidence: Such a heating and cooling cycle induces oscillation varying α and β phases with respect to their volume fraction, during which the epitaxial growth of α leads to near complete globularization upon slow cooling

causal_relations_full.cause: high-density dislocation sub-structure inherent to martensitic laths

causal_relations_full.effect: development of sub-grain boundaries and grain growth
causal_relations_full.trigger_word: was viewed as the driving force for

causal_relations_full.cause: misfit interphase boundary cyclic phase transformation

causal_relations_full.effect: subsequent cooling leads to recurrence of lamellar α colony

The Ti6Al4V alloy samples were initially polished using silicon carbide (SiC) sandpaper.
Subsequently, the samples underwent mechanical polishing with a 0.25 μm diamond powder polishing agent until a mirror-like finish was achieved, with no visible scratches.
Then etching was performed using a solution consisting of 5 % HF, 10 % HNO3 and 85 % H2O, for approximately 10s.

evidence: Stefansson et al. indicated that the composition gradient between the αand βphase in titanium alloys induced the movement of theα/βinterface and globularized the αphase

causal_relations_full.effect: slow down the diffusion rate of elements, reduce the α/β interface mobility, decrease in globularization rate
causal_relations_full.trigger_word: will slow down, reduce, lead to
evidence: The low composition gradient will slow down the diffusion rate of elements, reduce the α/βinterface mobility, and ﬁnally lead to a decrease in globularization rate of the initial αphases.

causal_relations_full.effect: driving force for globularization of the α phases
evidence: the composition gradient is the main driving force for globularization of the αphases

causal_relations_full.effect: reduce the concentration gradient, weakening the driving force and mitigating the globularization process
evidence: the atom diffusion at high tem- perature will reduce the concentration gradient, thus weakening the driving force and mitigating the globularization process of the αlaths.

causal_relations_full.cause: ECR higher than 50°C/s but lower than 200°C/s
causal_relations_full.effect: fine basket-weave microstructure appears
evidence: if ECR is higher than 50°C/s but lower than 200°C/s, fine basket-weave microstructure appears

causal_relations_full.effect: coarse basket-weave microstructure forms
evidence: if ECR is in the range of 0.5 –50°C/s, coarse basket-weave microstructure forms

causal_relations_full.effect: widmanstatten microstructure emerges
evidence: if ECR is lower than 0.5°C/s, widmanstatten microstructure emerges

causal_relations_full.effect: initial martensitic α' phases decomposed, acicular α phases coarsened, colony and globular α phases formed
evidence: After 980°C CHT, the initial martensitic α' phases were decomposed, and the acicular α phases coarsened. The colony and globular α phases were formed

causal_relations_full.effect: formation of transformed β
evidence: The formation of the transformed β was caused by the precipitation of supersaturated α phases

causal_relations_full.effect: α phases partially transformed into β phase, colony α phases disappeared
evidence: During the subsequent solution heat treatment, α phases partially transformed into the β phase, while the colony α phases disappeared

causal_relations_full.cause: air cooling after solution heat treatment
causal_relations_full.effect: fine secondary α phases emerged from high-temperature β phases
evidence: fine secondary α phases emerged from the high-temperature β phases in the following air cooling process

causal_relations_full.cause: higher β phase fraction in 980/C14C CHT +Solution samples
causal_relations_full.effect: lower strength compared to 980/C14C CHT samples despite fine secondary α phases
evidence: This may be related to the higher βphase fraction in the 980/C14C CHT þSolution samples

causal_relations_full.cause: appearance of fine secondary α phases
causal_relations_full.effect: enhances work hardening rate
evidence: the appearance of the ﬁne secondary αphases enhances the work hardening rate of the deposited sample

causal_relations_full.cause: mixed brittle and ductile fracture mode (cleavage planes and dimples)
causal_relations_full.effect: low elongation and no significant necking in as-built sample
evidence: This is also the main reason why the as-built sample exhibits low elongation and there is no signi ﬁcant necking during the room-temperature tensile process.

causal_relations_full.effect: transition in fracture mode from mixed to ductile
evidence: No cleavage plane can be observed on the fractured heat-treated samples, which indicates the transition in the fracture mode from mixed to ductile after the heat treatment.

causal_relations_full.cause: existence of α phases with various orientations
causal_relations_full.effect: interactions between adjacent α phases causing them to bend simultaneously during deformation
evidence: due to the existence of the αphases with various orientations, there are interactions between the adjacent αphases, which caused them to be bent simultaneously during the deformation process.

causal_relations_full.effect: better plastic deformability, leading to more severe deformation of β phases prior to fracture
evidence: The coarsen αlaths exhibited better plastic deformability, and hence the βphases between the adjacent αlaths experienced more severe deformation prior to fracture

causal_relations_full.effect: significant hindering effect on dislocation movements
evidence: the transformed βphase displayed signi ﬁcant hindering effect on the dislocation movements.

causal_relations_full.cause: formation of secondary α phases after 980/C14C CHT +Solution
causal_relations_full.effect: further hinder dislocation movements
evidence: The formation of secondary αphases after the 980/C14C CHT þSolution is expected to further hinder the dislocation movements.

causal_relations_full.cause: refined β phases with small width after 980/C14C CHT +Solution
causal_relations_full.effect: more likely to deform and result in lower stress concentration
evidence: The re ﬁnedβphases (with small width) are also more likely to deform and result in lower stress concentration.

parameters.deformation_type: single phase region rolling and dual phase region rolling

evidence: a duplex-step rolling deformation, comprising single phase region rolling and dual phase region rolling
Rolling

Hot rolling deformation was conducted on the as-cast Ti-55511 alloy and can be divided into two stages: single-phase region rolling at 1000 °C with a thickness reduction of 60% and dual-phase region rolling at 700 °C with a thickness reduction of 30%.
Fig. 2.(a) True stress-true strain curves of the present Ti-55511 alloy at room-temperature after rolling and different heat treatments.
Fig. 3.SEM images of the Ti-55511 alloy after different rolling and different heat treatments: (a, b) R0, (c, d) RSA, (e, f) RDA1 and (g, h) RDA2 specimens.
rolling deformation and was adopted as the original sample
a high temperature heat treatment (930 •C) for a short time (3 min)
aging at a lower temperature (600 •C) for 120 min
The specimens undergoing single-step aging with the temperature of 600 °C for 120 min were named as RSA.

The specimens after the first duplex-step aging process including an aging step at a higher temperature (830 °C) for 120 min and a following aging step at a lower temperature (600 °C) for 120 min were named as RDA1.
the second duplex-step aging process adopted a higher temperature (930 °C) with an extremely short time of 3 min instead of at 830 °C for 120 min in the first step aging.
Fig. 2.(a) True stress-true strain curves of the present Ti-55511 alloy at room-temperature after rolling and different heat treatments.
Fig. 3.SEM images of the Ti-55511 alloy after different rolling and different heat treatments: (a, b) R0, (c, d) RSA, (e, f) RDA1 and (g, h) RDA2 specimens.

duplex-step aging processes
830 °C was chosen as the first-stage aging temperature
second-stage aging at 600 °C
Tensile testing results show that
Uniaxial tensile tests were performed to characterize the mechanical properties of the specimens at room temperature using an Instron 3369 testing machine.
Flat dog-bone-shaped specimens with a gauge length of 9.5 mm and a width of 2 mm were cut from the rolled and heat-treated plates parallel to the rolling direction (RD).
three specimens were tested for each condition.
A scanning electron microscope (SEM, FEI Nova NanoSEM230)
a transmission electron microscope (TEM, FEI Titan G2 60–300)
The fracture surfaces of the tensile fractured samples were characterized to further understand the fracture mechanisms.

properties_full.condition: after HIP compared to after sintering
evidence: The plasticity of the alloy after HIP increased 4.3 times compared to the state after sintering
The nominal density of the cast alloy is 4.67 g /cm3 [36].
After HIP at 750 °C and 160 MPa, the material had rather low yield strength (600 MPa).
Increasing the HIP pressure by 20 MPa significantly prolongs the stage of uniform deformation on tensile curve (up to 10% elongation, Fig. 9a).
The true stress-true strain curve is characterized by exponent n =0.91 and two stages (Fig. 9b).
The fracture of the material in the outer zone was less ductile in tension than in the inner zone.

causal_relations_full.cause: application of an additional short-time annealing to the solution heat treated and quenched material
causal_relations_full.effect: pronounced increase in strength

causal_relations_full.cause: increasing the solution heat treatment temperature
causal_relations_full.effect: higher strength and reduced ductility
evidence: Increasing the solution heat treatment temperature resulted in higher strength and reduced ductility

causal_relations_full.effect: microstructure (e.g. phase fractions, interface characteristics, chemical phase composition) and deformation behavior
evidence: The solution heat treatment temperature is the crucial parameter influencing microstructure (e.g. phase fractions, interface characteristics, chemical phase composition) and, consequently, the deformation behavior

causal_relations_full.cause: supersaturated condition of hexagonal α’-martensite compared to hcp αp-phase
causal_relations_full.effect: slight differences in c and a lattice parameters as well as c/a ratio of the unit cell
evidence: This observation is attributed to the supersaturated condition of hexagonal α’-martensite compared to the hcp αp-phase, which is accompanied by slight differences in c and a lattice parameters as well as the c/a ratio of the unit cell.

causal_relations_full.effect: introduces internal stresses
evidence: a flatter levelling of the αp/α’ peaks in the STQ-state, extending to both lower and higher diffraction angles, indicates that the martensite introduces internal stresses.

causal_relations_full.cause: relaxation of α’ martensite and/or a chemical change
causal_relations_full.effect: narrowing and steeper levelling of α/α’-peaks from STQ to STA
evidence: the narrowing must be attributable to either the relaxation of α’ martensite and/or a chemical change.

causal_relations_full.cause: increase of the solubility of Al in β-phase with increasing TST
causal_relations_full.effect: increasing Al-content and decreasing V-content within βtrans
evidence: This observation of an increasing Al-content and a decreasing V-content within βtrans is again related to the thermodynamics of the Ti-6Al-4V system and is due to the increase of the solubility of Al in β-phase with increasing TST

causal_relations_full.cause: differing AR microstructures: thin, partially textured Ti-6Al-4V sheet material vs. more homogeneous, less textured ingot Ti-6Al-4V material
causal_relations_full.effect: slight deviation of the results of this study with that from Chong et al. [25] regarding the correlation between αp/βtrans interfacial linedensity as function of TST
evidence: The slight deviation of the results of this study with that from Chong et al. [25] regarding the correlation between αp/βtrans interfacial linedensity as function of TST can thus be attributed to the differing AR microstructures: thin, partially textured Ti-6Al-4V sheet material vs. more homogeneous, less textured ingot Ti-6Al-4V material.

causal_relations_full.cause: morphology of the phases (bilamellar α+α’ vs. bimodal α+α’)
causal_relations_full.effect: affects the αp/βtrans interfacial linedensity and the strain distribution during deformation, which in turn affects the trend of UEL values as a function of TST
evidence: Chong et al. [32] additionally showed that the morphology of the phases - whether bilamellar α+α’ (α-lamellae, surrounded by βtrans) or bimodal α+α’ (globular α-grains adjacent to βtrans-grains) - affects the αp/βtrans interfacial linedensity and the strain distribution during deformation. This, in turn, affects the trend of UEL values as a function of TST.

causal_relations_full.cause: V-content in βtrans decreases with increasing TST
causal_relations_full.effect: hardening process is crucially influenced
evidence: the hardening process is likely crucially influenced by either the V-content in βtrans, which decreases with increasing TST

causal_relations_full.cause: segregation, clustering, or precipitation of V
causal_relations_full.effect: significant increase in strength by contributing to inhibition of dislocation movement
evidence: Segregation, clustering, or precipitation of V may be reasonable for this significant increase in strength by contributing to the inhibition of dislocation movement

causal_relations_full.cause: decomposition of metastable α'-martensite towards α+β
causal_relations_full.effect: higher strength compared to α/α'-microstructures
evidence: they observed a decomposition of the metastable α'-martensite towards α+β using SEM, and the resulting α+(α'dec)-microstructures were characterized by a higher strength compared to the corresponding α/α'-microstructures

causal_relations_full.effect: clustering of β-stabilizer V occurs at a very fine scale, corresponding to an early stage of martensite decomposition
evidence: it is plausible that after the short annealing times of only 180s in this study, clustering of the β-stabilizer V occurs at a very fine scale, corresponding to an early stage of martensite decomposition

causal_relations_full.cause: small crystallographic change from hcp to bcc
causal_relations_full.effect: narrower α/α'-peaks and very small β-peaks in Fig.5c
evidence: This assumption aligns well with the narrower α/α'-peaks observed for the STA-state compared to the STQ-state, as well as the very small β-peaks in Fig.5c), suggesting that a small crystallographic change from hcp to bcc has already occurred

causal_relations_full.cause: β-phase in STA-state has larger lattice parameter
causal_relations_full.effect: lower V-content than in AR-state
evidence: Since the STA-β-peak occurs at a smaller diffraction angle than the AR-β-peak, it can be inferred that the β-phase in the STA-state has a larger lattice parameter, which indicates a lower V-content than in AR-state

causal_relations_full.effect: increase in βtrans-fraction from 41% to 47.7%
evidence: With increasing tST, an increase in βtrans-fraction from 41% (tST=90s) to 47.7% (tST=540s) was determined

Laser powder bed fusion
LPBF-built
Porous structure samples of the Ti–25Nb –3Zr–3Mo –1Sn (wt.%) alloy were prepared through the SLM, also known as LPBF technique.
The LPBF machine is equipped with a 200 W Yb: YAG fiber laser capable of producing a laser beam with a focal spot size of 40 μm.
Each powder layer had a thickness of 50 μm, and the hatch spacing was set at 100 μm.
The input power was fixed at 200 W, and the scanning speed was maintained at 1000 mm/s.

Fatigue tests were conducted on topologically optimized (TO) porous structures in both as-printed and heat-treated states, with a comparison between TO and rhombic dodecahedron (RD) structures.

After fatigue testing, EBSD, SEM, and TEM analyses were conducted on TO structures to gain insights into the fatigue performance of β titanium alloy and phase transformation in topological porous structures.

figure_grounding_full.description: shows elemental distribution on carburized surfaces of TA1 and TC21 alloys
evidence: the exposed middle part was dark structure (Fig. 5(a)). O element was enriched in the thin light gray layer, while Ti and C elements were enriched in the middle part (Fig. 5(b)–(d))

As for the TC21 alloy carburized at 1100 °C, the surface exhibited the morphology of tortuous lumps with granules at the boundaries (Fig. 5(e)). Ti element did not presented a tortuous lump-shaped distribution, since both tortuous lump and boundary areas contained high content of Ti element (Fig. 5(f)). However, O and C elements presented an obvious lump-shaped distribution, in which O element was distributed mainly along the structure of the lumps (Fig. 5(g)), while C element was a little more distributed in the boundary areas (Fig. 5(h)). The stabilized α-Ti phase alloying element Al was primarily distributed on the tortuous lumps (Fig. 5(i)), whereas stabilized β-Ti phase alloying element Nb was distributed in the boundary areas (Fig. 5(j)).

experimental_conditions_full.test_type: texture evolution and mechanical properties investigation
evidence: effect of LSP treatment on the texture evolution and mechanical properties of Ti e6Ale4V alloy was investigated
interlayer LSP treatment significantly improved the micro-hardness
interlayer LSP treatment significantly improved the ... tensile properties of DED specimens
fatigue crack growth (FCG) behavior of long cracks in Tie6Ale4V fabricated by high-power DED was also investigated

The XRD data was collected in reflection mode using the PANalytical Empyrean diffractometer with Cu-Kα radiation (λ=1.54056 Å). A scanning speed of 3°/min was used to record the diffraction data from 30° to 90°.
The scanning range is 174 mm × 135 mm.
The Vickers microhardness of specimens before and after EST was measured using a hardness tester (HUAYIN HV-1000A, China) with 500g loading and 10 s dwell time.

Compression experiments were performed on a testing machine (SANS-CMT5205) with a compression rate of 0.1 mm/min at room temperature. The compressive stress-strain curves were obtained following the GB/T 7314-2005 (China) test standard.
The fracture morphology of specimens after compression tests were characterized by SEM to analyze the fracture mode.

evidence: Specimens for SEM/EDS/EBSD were hot-mounted in Struers Polyfast resin using a Struers Citopress-20. The 30 mm diameter mounts were ground and polished on a Struers Tegramin-20 with final polishing carried out with a solution consisting of 100 mL oxide polishing suspension (OPS), 500 mL water, 2 mL hydrogen peroxide and 2 mL ammonia for 5 min. An FEI Helios NanoLab G3 CX dual beam field emission gun -focused ion beam microscope was used for electron imaging and EDS. Secondary and back-scatter electron imaging was carried out at 10 kV and 11 nA.

causal_relations_full.cause: heat transfer primarily in weld zone, lower removal temperature in TMAZ
causal_relations_full.effect: number of dissolved hphase particles reduced in TMAZ, insoluble S phase grows

evidence: Tensile properties were evaluated using a Z100 from ZwickRoell.

Round tensile bars with a diameter of 6 mm and a gauge length of 30 mm (fabricated according to DIN 50125A) were tested according to EN-ISO 6892-1B in vertical and horizontal directions from both heat-treated conditions.
For hardness testing, profiles and mappings with the distances between the indentations of 0.25 mm were obtained close to the fracture surfaces.
Vickers hardness with 100 g loads was used with a DuraScan 70 G5 from ZwickRoell.
Phase identification and quantification based on crystallographic structural information was conducted using HE-XRD.
The mean photon energy was 102.8 keV, and the beam size was adjusted to 1 ×1 mm2.
2D diffraction images were captured using a PerkinElmer XRD 1621 flat panel detector with an exposure time of 0.2 ×30 (summed exposures).

The diffusion bonding process was performed in a high vacuum furnace. The temperature of the furnace was raised from room temperature to 900 •C at a rate of 10 •C/min, and held isothermally for 6 min. Subsequently, the samples were cooled down to 400 •C at a rate of 10 •C/min and finally furnace cooled to room temperature.
The prepared Ti6Al4V/304 SS micro-laminated composites were cut by wire cut electrical discharge machining in the direction of cross section.

One of the two peened walls was subjected to post-build standard β annealing heat treatment to 1050 •C at 0.3 •C/s for 30 minutes, followed by cooling at ~ 0.1 •C/s.

after the post-build β heat treatment (Fig. 4c), no HAZ or fusion boundary bands are apparent as the whole wall has been uniformly heat treated and retransformed with longer thermal exposure above the β transus, where solute diffusion is relatively rapid. parameters.image_analysis: Image-J software, line intersection method, ASTM E112

evidence: Microstructure analysis was conducted on samples sectioned from the wall centres in the WD-ND and ND-TD planes (Fig. 1a) using optical light microscope and scanning electron microscopes (SEM).
The optical samples were etched with Kroll ’s Reagent.

For α lath width measurements, multiple secondary electron SEM images were taken across the WD-ND plane and then processed using Image-J software by the line intersection method, according to ASTM E112 standard [35].

parameters.instrument: Thermo Fisher Apreo C, field emission gun SEM, Oxford Instruments Symmetry 2 EBSD detector

evidence: EBSD maps were acquired in the ND-TD plane using a Thermo Fisher Apreo C, field emission gun SEM, equipped with an Oxford Instruments ' Symmetry 2 EBSD detector.
The acquisition was done with an accelerating voltage of 20kV and a beam current of 51nA at an indexing rate of 1800 Hz, with a step size of 10 μm.
EBSD maps were processed and β grain size data were extracted using Oxford instruments ’ AZtecCrystal software.

The parent β phase maps were reconstructed from the α phase data using the software developed by Davies and Wynne [36]with a maximum disorientation of 2º from a particular α variant accepted, and up to a 3º divergence from the Burgers orientation relationship (BOR) as a part of the same parent β grain.

parameters.machine: Instron electromechanical test machine with 100kN load cell

evidence: Flat, dog-bone shaped, tensile specimens (Fig. 1b), 5mm thick and 6mm wide, were extracted using wire electric discharge machining (W-EDM) from the walls in two orientations: horizontal (H) and vertical (V), corresponding to the loading axis being in parallel or perpendicular to the deposited layers (Fig. 1).
The sample size (Fig. 1b) and testing procedure follow the ASTM E8 standard [33].
Tensile testing was performed on an Instron electromechanical test machine with 100kN load cell, at a crosshead speed of 2mm/min, at room temperature.
Extensional deformation was measured using a laser non-contact video extensometer over a gauge length of 25mm.
Two specimens were tested for each process condition and material orientation.
High cycle fatigue (HCF) tests by rotating bending load were conducted using round dog-bone specimens with two material orientations (Figs. 1c, 1a).
Fig. 4d-f show higher magnification secondary electron SEM images of the typical transformation microstructure found within the transformed β grains.

microstructure_full.description: darker layer in the HAZ band in the as-built and the peened samples, caused by coarsening of the α laths and greater interphase solute partitioning
evidence: walls that were not heat treated, heat affected zone (HAZ) bands, can be seen periodically spaced along the build direction (Fig. 4a and b).

They form in the temperature gradient under the heat source when the previous layer is reheated to a peak temperature between ~ 800 •C and the β transus (~1000 •C) [46,47] . This results in coarsening of the α laths and greater interphase solute partitioning, which appears as a darker layer in the HAZ band in the as-built and the peened samples.

evidence: large single-variant lamellar α colonies were produced with a coarser α lath width.
increased α lath width

microstructure_full.description: thin GBα single-variant colonies
evidence: slip concentration in thin GBα single-variant colonies

microstructure_full.description: strong ⊑001⊒β // ND texture in columnar β grains; reduced texture strength after peening
evidence: strong ⊑001⊒β // ND texture in columnar β grains
significantly reduced texture strength

causal_relations_full.effect: refines the grain structure by inducing recrystallization
evidence: interpass peening effectively refines the grain structure by inducing recrystallization

causal_relations_full.effect: isotropic properties and increased strength without compromising fatigue crack growth resistance
evidence: resulting in isotropic properties and increased strength without compromising fatigue crack growth resistance

causal_relations_full.cause: post-deposition annealing above the β-transus temperature (β annealing)
causal_relations_full.effect: significantly reduces the fatigue crack growth rate by an order of magnitude
evidence: post-deposition annealing above the β-transus temperature (β annealing) significantly reduces the fatigue crack growth rate by an order of magnitude

causal_relations_full.cause: formation of coarse single-variant lamellar colonies
causal_relations_full.effect: promotes crack path branching and deviation, enhancing fatigue crack growth performance
evidence: The formation of coarse single-variant lamellar colonies promotes crack path branching and deviation, enhancing fatigue crack growth performance

causal_relations_full.cause: Combining in-process grain refinement via peening with post-process β annealing
causal_relations_full.effect: further increases the threshold stress intensity factor by 2.5 times
evidence: Combining in-process grain refinement via peening with post-process β annealing further increases the threshold stress intensity factor by 2.5 times

causal_relations_full.cause: reheating of the previous layer to a peak temperature between ~800°C and the β transus (~1000°C)
causal_relations_full.effect: coarsening of the α laths and greater interphase solute partitioning, which appears as a darker layer in the HAZ band
evidence: They form in the temperature gradient under the heat source when the previous layer is reheated to a peak temperature between ~ 800 •C and the β transus (~1000 •C) [46,47] . This results in coarsening of the α laths and greater interphase solute partitioning, which appears as a darker layer in the HAZ band in the as-built and the peened samples.

causal_relations_full.cause: peak temperature just below the β transus (~1000°C) when D5% α remains
causal_relations_full.effect: finer single α variant lamellar colony microstructure formed by sympathetic nucleation from surviving α plates on cooling
evidence: At the top of each HAZ band a thin white etched layer is seen, caused by a finer single α variant lamellar colony microstructure that is formed where the peak temperature was just below the β transus (~1000•C) when D5% α remains and α is sympathetically nucleated from the surviving α plates on cooling [35–37].

causal_relations_full.cause: post-build β heat treatment with longer thermal exposure above the β transus
causal_relations_full.effect: no HAZ or fusion boundary bands apparent
evidence: after the post-build β heat treatment (Fig. 4c), no HAZ or fusion boundary bands are apparent as the whole wall has been uniformly heat treated and retransformed with longer thermal exposure above the β transus, where solute diffusion is relatively rapid.

causal_relations_full.effect: more prominent grain boundary layer of α allotriomorphs (GB α)
evidence: For this microstructure the slower cooling rate has led to a more prominent grain boundary layer of α allotriomorphs (GB α), which reveals the β grain structure more clearly than in the other walls.

causal_relations_full.cause: reheating during subsequent passes of the heat source
causal_relations_full.effect: coarsening of the average lath width if there is overlap of the HAZ bands
evidence: This reheating can cause coarsening of the average lath width if there is overlap of the HAZ bands, as seen in Fig. 3a [48].

causal_relations_full.cause: large heat source and thicker layer deposition associated with the WAAM process
causal_relations_full.effect: slower cooling rates of about 30 ºC/s
evidence: The large heat source and thicker layer deposition associated with the WAAM process result in slower cooling rates of about 30 ºC/s [49] compared to other AM processes

causal_relations_full.cause: crack initiating from a large colony
causal_relations_full.effect: shorter fatigue life
evidence: Crack initiating from a large colony have lower resistance to cyclic slip, resulting in a shorter fatigue life

causal_relations_full.cause: variations in the local microstructure and its crystallographic orientation around the crack initiation site
causal_relations_full.effect: fatigue data scatter
evidence: variations in the local microstructure and its crystallographic orientation around the crack initiation site are primary contributors to fatigue data scatter in WAAM built Ti64

causal_relations_full.cause: higher distribution of the Schmid factor for the pyramidal slip system around the crack initiation site
causal_relations_full.effect: higher fatigue life
evidence: samples with a higher distribution of the Schmid factor for the pyramidal slip system around the crack initiation site exhibited higher fatigue life

causal_relations_full.cause: strong crystallographic texture of the colony microstructure
causal_relations_full.effect: scatter in the fatigue test data
evidence: the strong crystallographic texture of the colony microstructure likely contributed to the scatter in the fatigue test data

causal_relations_full.cause: slip concentration in thin GBα single-variant colonies
causal_relations_full.effect: lower ductility in horizontal direction and earlier fracture
evidence: ductility in the horizontal direction was lower than the vertical direction due to the slip concentration in thin GBα single-variant colonies, leading to earlier fracture.

causal_relations_full.cause: strong ⊑001⊒β // ND texture in columnar β grains
causal_relations_full.effect: slip concentration
evidence: The slip concentration was the cause of strong ⊑001⊒β // ND texture in columnar β grains.

causal_relations_full.cause: weaker crystallographic texture and α lamellae orientation
causal_relations_full.effect: reduced anisotropy in ductility and increased yield strength by 10%
evidence: Interpass peening has reduced the anisotropy in the ductility and increased the yield strength by 10% due to weaker crystallographic texture and α lamellae orientation.

causal_relations_full.effect: slightly decreased yield strength in annealed condition
evidence: the yield strength was slightly decreased in the annealed condition due to increased α lath width

causal_relations_full.cause: similar α lath width in as-built and peened materials
causal_relations_full.effect: high cycle fatigue performance ranking same as tensile strength
evidence: High cycle fatigue performance followed the same ranking as the tensile strength, which can be attributed to the similar α lath width in the as-built and peened materials.

causal_relations_full.cause: larger α lath width and colony microstructure after β annealing
causal_relations_full.effect: decreased fatigue life
evidence: Fatigue life was decreased after β annealing due to larger α lath width and colony microstructure.

causal_relations_full.cause: multi-variant α colonies and similar α lath width in as-built and peened samples
causal_relations_full.effect: comparable crack growth rates
evidence: Multi-variant α colonies and similar α lath width in the as-built and peened samples have resulted in comparable crack growth rates.

causal_relations_full.cause: post-build β annealing creating large single-variant colonies
causal_relations_full.effect: reduced crack growth rates by an order of magnitude and increased threshold stress intensity factor range by 2.5 times
evidence: post-build β annealing, which created large single-variant colonies, has reduced crack growth rates by an order of magnitude and increased the threshold stress intensity factor range by 2.5 times due to significant crack path deviation and branching.

The μCT analyses were performed using an FF35 CT system (Comet Yxlon, Germany). Given the large number of scans needed to inspect the 53 samples, a compromise between the acquisition time and scan quality was found, resulting in 1500 projections with an X-ray tube voltage of 140 kV, a tube current of 50 μA, and an exposure time of 475 ms over 3 frames integration. To limit the beam hardening effect, a 0.3 mm copper filter was used. The projection data were then corrected and reconstructed using the Siemens CERA software, with a resulting voxel size of 8 μm.

quasi-static compression specimens were cut into 4 mm × 5 mm size (Standard: GB/T 7314 2017) and test was performed at a very low strain rate of 10^-3/s
Nano-indentation test was carried out using the Nano Indenter XPs system (MTS, Inc.) equipped with a standard Berkovich diamond tip indenter

abrasion test was escorted by using an auto polishing equipment (EI-GP2T) containing facilities of a clock and anti-clockwise rotation of the polishing wheel and sample holder, respectively. Colloidal silica paste on the special polishing cloth was used as an abrasion medium. Whereas the speed of the clockwise rotating polishing wheel, anti-clockwise rotating sample holder head, and the force on the specimen were maintained at 400 rpm, 100 rpm, and 5 N, respectively
A three-point bend test was conducted at room temperature using an electromechanical universal testing machine (EUTM INSTRON5989)
SEM (A ZEISS Supra55) embedded with EBSD (Nordlys Nano, Oxford) and EDS (Oxford) detectors
HR-TEM (JEM-1200EX)
EBSD tests were performed at step size ranging from 0.2 to 3 µm by maintaining the specimen surface at angle of 70° to the electron beam
Specimens for EBSD and HR-TEM were prepared with the help of electro-polishing (5% perchloric acid, 95% acetic acid, voltage 40 V)
Focused Ion Beam (FIB, FIB200, FEI) technique

The laser beam movement per time step is set to 30 µm, equivalent to the fine mesh size.

causal_relations_full.effect: pronounced increase in strength
evidence: the application of an additional short-time annealing to the solution heat treated and quenched material resulted in a pronounced increase in strength

causal_relations_full.effect: hardening of the martensitically transformed prior β-grains
evidence: which was attributed to the hardening of the martensitically transformed prior β-grains

causal_relations_full.effect: higher strength and reduced ductility
evidence: Increasing the solution heat treatment temperature resulted in higher strength and reduced ductility

causal_relations_full.effect: microstructure (phase fractions, interface characteristics, chemical phase composition) and deformation behavior
evidence: The solution heat treatment temperature is the crucial parameter influencing microstructure (e.g. phase fractions, interface characteristics, chemical phase composition) and, consequently, the deformation behavior

causal_relations_full.cause: supersaturated condition of hexagonal α'-martensite compared to hcp αp-phase
causal_relations_full.effect: peak-broadening and shifting of αp/α' peak maxima
evidence: This observation is attributed to the supersaturated condition of hexagonal α'-martensite compared to the hcp αp-phase

causal_relations_full.effect: introduces internal stresses
evidence: a flatter levelling of the αp/α' peaks in the STQ-state, extending to both lower and higher diffraction angles, indicates that the martensite introduces internal stresses

causal_relations_full.cause: relaxation of α' martensite and/or a chemical change
causal_relations_full.effect: narrowing and steeper levelling of α/α'-peaks from STQ to STA
evidence: the narrowing must be attributable to either the relaxation of α' martensite and/or a chemical change

causal_relations_full.cause: increase of the solubility of Al in β-phase with increasing TST
causal_relations_full.effect: increasing Al-content and decreasing V-content within βtrans
evidence: This observation of an increasing Al-content and a decreasing V-content within βtrans is again related to the thermodynamics of the Ti-6Al-4V system and is due to the increase of the solubility of Al in β-phase with increasing TST

causal_relations_full.cause: differing AR microstructures: thin, partially textured Ti-6Al-4V sheet material vs. more homogeneous, less textured ingot Ti-6Al-4V material
causal_relations_full.effect: slight deviation of the results of this study with that from Chong et al. [25] regarding the correlation between αp/βtrans interface linedensity as function of TST
evidence: The slight deviation of the results of this study with that from Chong et al. [25] regarding the correlation between αp/βtrans interface linedensity as function of TST can thus be attributed to the differing AR microstructures: thin, partially textured Ti-6Al-4V sheet material vs. more homogeneous, less textured ingot Ti-6Al-4V material

causal_relations_full.cause: morphology of the phases (bilamellar α+α’ vs. bimodal α+α’)
causal_relations_full.effect: affects the αp/βtrans interface linedensity and the strain distribution during deformation, which in turn affects the trend of UEL values
evidence: Chong et al. [32] additionally showed that the morphology of the phases - whether bilamellar α+α’ (α-lamellae, surrounded by βtrans) or bimodal α+α’ (globular α-grains adjacent to βtrans-grains) - affects the αp/βtrans interface linedensity and the strain distribution during deformation. This, in turn, affects the trend of UEL values as a function of TST

causal_relations_full.cause: V-content in βtrans decreasing with increasing TST
causal_relations_full.effect: hardening process
evidence: the hardening process is likely crucially influenced by either the V-content in βtrans, which decreases with increasing TST

causal_relations_full.effect: clustering of β-stabilizer V at a very fine scale
evidence: it is plausible that after the short annealing times of only 180s in this study, clustering of the β-stabilizer V occurs at a very fine scale

causal_relations_full.cause: decomposition of metastable α'-martensite towards α+β
causal_relations_full.effect: higher strength
evidence: they observed a decomposition of the metastable α'-martensite towards α+β using SEM, and the resulting α+(α'dec)-microstructures were characterized by a higher strength compared to the corresponding α+α'-microstructures
